distribution.name: paris_flood_dataset.csv
distribution.description:
## General file description:

- Format: comma-separated values (CSV).
- Encoding: UTF-8 encoded (no BOM expected).
- Delimiter: comma (`,`)
- Null/missing values: represented as empty fields, treat empty string as missing values
- 53332 lines, 15 features. 125+ years of continuous data.
- 19 date gaps (days with missing observations): mostly around the new year's Eve, 31 Dec and/or 01 Jan.
- Since 2006 two records per day are available (double monitoring), prefer the `F700000103` record.
- In France the flood alert is raised at various stages by various administrations; A threshold has been used to compute the flood alert column: `6000` mm. It relates to the closure of the [last areas to the public](https://www.paris.fr/pages/crues-5472) in Paris by the city council (Voie Mazas / Souterrain Citroën Cévennes).
- Water levels can be subzero.
> The water level of the Seine is measured continuously on the Austerlitz scale, located near the bridge of the same name. The "zero" point on this scale has been set at 25.92 m NGF-IGN69 (NGF = General Level of France established for metropolitan France in 1969, with zero determined by the Marseille tide gauge).

## Data structure

Final CSV columns have been extracted through the Hub'Eau API in French and manually translated into English.
Labels for production, validation and quality have also been translated into English. 

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---:|---|
| **location_code** | string | Station location identifier |
| **station_code** | string | Unique station identifier |
| **record_date** | date (YYYY-MM-DD) | Observation date |
| **water_level_mm** | float | Daily maximum water level (millimetres) |
| **data_production_date** | timestamp (ISO 8601 UTC) | When the record was produced |
| **validation_status_code** | integer | Numeric validation state code |
| **validation_status** | string | Human-readable validation state  |
| **production_method_code** | integer | Numeric code for production method |
| **production_method** | string | Method description (e.g., "measured") |
| **quality_code** | integer | Numeric quality indicator |
| **quality_assessment** | string | Human-readable quality label |
| **longitude** | float | Station longitude (decimal degrees) |
| **latitude** | float | Station latitude (decimal degrees) |
| **hubeau_elab_code** | string | Hubeau metric code (HIXnJ = daily max height) |
| **flood_alert** | boolean | Flood alert flag (True/False) |

## Data sample

```csv
location_code,station_code,record_date,water_level_mm,data_production_date,validation_status_code,validation_status,production_method_code,production_method,quality_code,quality_assessment,longitude,latitude,hubeau_elab_code,flood_alert
F7000001,F700000109,1900-01-02,1300.0,2025-06-17T09:27:10Z,16,validated,0,measured,20,good,2.365515502,48.845409133,HIXnJ,False
```

## Example usage (Python)

### Load dataset

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("paris_flood_dataset.csv", parse_dates=["record_date"])
df.head()
```

### Analyze a single station

```python
station_df = df[df["station_code"] == "F700000109"]
station_df.set_index("record_date")["water_level_mm"].plot()
```

### Detect missing days

```python
full_range = pd.date_range(
    df["record_date"].min(),
    df["record_date"].max(),
    freq="D"
)
missing = full_range.difference(df["record_date"])
len(missing)
```

### Station coverage summary

```python
coverage = (
    df.groupby("station_code")["record_date"]
      .agg(["min", "max", "count"])
)
```

### Compute monthly averages

```python
df["month"] = df["record_date"].dt.to_period("M")

monthly_avg = (
    df.groupby(["station_code", "month"])
      ["water_level_mm"]
      .mean()
)
```

### Detect extreme events (top 1%)

```python
threshold = df["water_level_mm"].quantile(0.99)
extreme_events = df[df["water_level_mm"] >= threshold]
```

field.name: water_level_mm
field.description: Observed value: daily max water level (in mm)

name: Paris flood dataset
description:
![](https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/kaggle-user-content/o/inbox%2F27942110%2F15bc77397c132125b68edc6a185710fa%2Flarge.jpg?generation=1773093658623620&alt=media)
*The Seine rose gray and terrible, and the city that had seemed eternal drowned quietly as there was nothing to do but watch it happen.* (Paris, 1910. Author unknown.)

This dataset contains **daily maximum water level observations for the Paris Austerlitz area**, collected from multiple hydrometric monitoring stations via the official [Hub'Eau Hydrométrie API](https://hubeau.eaufrance.fr/page/api-hydrometrie). 
It provides a consolidated, chronologically ordered time series of the river water height measurements across several decades.

> The data collecting script is available on [GitHub](https://github.com/brooks-code/paris-flood-dataset).

## What is it about?

- Measurement type: **maximum** daily water height
- Unit: millimeters (mm)  
- Temporal resolution: daily (One observation per station per available day)  
- Temporal coverage: january 1900 to February 2026
- Format: CSV

### Potential use cases:

These measurements are widely used in:

- Flood monitoring
- Flood pattern detection
- Hydrological & flood forecasting modeling  
- Seasonal trend analysis  
- Climate impact & variability research
- Time-series analysis and prediction (ARIMA, LSTM, Prophet)
- Extreme value analysis (GEV, POT)
- Machine learning forecasting
- Anomaly detection
- Infrastructure risk modeling

## Geographic coverage

- Country: France (FR).
- Region: La Seine à Paris, Pont d'Austerlitz. 
- Official monitoring network: National hydrometric stations  
- Each row preserves the original station identifier  

## Temporal coverage

Coverage varies by station. The dataset preserves:

- Real missing days  
- Station-specific coverage differences  

No artificial interpolation has been applied.

```python
    "F700000109",  # before 1966
    "F700000110",  # 1966-1973
    "F700000111",  # 1974-1989
    "F700000102",  # 1990-2006 and after (unofficial)
    "F700000103",  # after 2006 (official)
```

## Data processing & quality control

The dataset was generated through a structured data pipeline:

1. Concurrent data collection per station
2. Date-based pagination for complete historical retrieval
3. Multi-station merging
4. Duplicate date detection
5. Global missing-date detection
6. Chronological sorting

Data integrity was preserved: no smoothing or gap-filling was applied.

## Limitations

> Station coverage varies over time.
> After 2006, two stations cover the area, per the [hydroportail](https://www.hydro.eaufrance.fr/stationhydro/F700000109/fiche) recommendation, it is advised to consider `F700000103` in priority.

- No meteorological covariates included
- No watershed metadata included

### Keywords

Hydrology dataset • River water levels • Flood prediction data • Time-series forecasting • Climate change analysis • Environmental data • Extreme value analysis • Daily river height • Hydrometric stations • Machine learning dataset

## License

Provided under a [CC0](https://creativecommons.org/public-domain/cc0/) license.

## Acknowledgments

The [Hub'Eau](https://hubeau.eaufrance.fr/) and [Vigicrue](https://www.vigicrues.gouv.fr/) teams.
